topics_flat: Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Prime numbers
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Factorization techniques
Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Counting two ways
prompt:
Determine all positive integers $n \ge 2$ which satisfy the following condition: For all integers $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$ such that their sum is not divisible by $n$, there exists an integer $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ such that none of the $n$ numbers

$$
a_i,\ a_i + a_{i+1},\ \dots,\ a_i + a_{i+1} + \dots + a_{i+n-1}
$$
is divisible by $n$, where we define $a_i = a_{i-n}$ for the case $i > n$.
response_text:
Let
\[
s_i=a_1+a_2+\cdots+a_i\qquad (s_0:=0),
\]
and indices be taken cyclically modulo \(n\).

We seek all \(n\ge 2\) such that:

> whenever \(a_1,\dots,a_n\in\mathbb Z\) have total sum \(S:=a_1+\cdots+a_n\not\equiv 0\pmod n\), there is some starting index \(i\) such that none of
\[
a_i,\ a_i+a_{i+1},\ \dots,\ a_i+\cdots+a_{i+n-1}
\]
is divisible by \(n\).

Equivalently, for some \(i\), the \(n\) cyclic partial sums of lengths \(1,\dots,n\) starting at \(i\) are all nonzero mod \(n\).

We will show that this happens exactly for prime \(n\).

---

## 1. Reformulation in terms of prefix sums

For fixed \(i\), the cyclic partial sums starting at \(i\) are
\[
s_i-s_{i-1},\ s_{i+1}-s_{i-1},\ \dots,\ s_{i+n-1}-s_{i-1},
\]
where indices are modulo \(n\), and \(s_n=S\).

Modulo \(n\), these are exactly
\[
x-s_{i-1}\qquad \text{for }x\in \{s_0,s_1,\dots,s_n\}\setminus\{s_{i-1}\}.
\]
So “none is divisible by \(n\)” means that no other prefix sum is congruent to \(s_{i-1}\pmod n\).

Thus the condition is equivalent to:

> among the \(n+1\) numbers \(s_0,s_1,\dots,s_n\) modulo \(n\), there exists some residue class that occurs exactly once, and that unique occurrence is some \(s_{i-1}\) with \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\).

Since \(s_n\equiv S\not\equiv 0\equiv s_0\pmod n\), we in fact only need:

> for every sequence \(s_0,s_1,\dots,s_n\) with \(s_0=0\), \(s_n\not\equiv 0\pmod n\), and arbitrary successive differences, some residue among \(s_0,\dots,s_{n-1}\) appears exactly once in the whole list \(s_0,\dots,s_n\).

---

## 2. Prime \(n\) works

Assume \(n=p\) prime.

Suppose, for contradiction, that the condition fails for some \(a_1,\dots,a_p\) with total sum
\[
S\not\equiv 0\pmod p.
\]
Then for every \(i\in\{0,1,\dots,p-1\}\), the residue \(s_i\) is not unique among \(s_0,\dots,s_p\). Since there are \(p+1\) numbers and only \(p\) residue classes mod \(p\), and every one of the first \(p\) terms \(s_0,\dots,s_{p-1}\) is repeated, each residue appearing among \(s_0,\dots,s_{p-1}\) must appear at least twice in the full list.

Let \(m_r\) be the number of indices \(j\in\{0,\dots,p\}\) with \(s_j\equiv r\pmod p\). Then:
- \(\sum_r m_r=p+1\),
- every residue appearing among \(s_0,\dots,s_{p-1}\) has \(m_r\ge 2\).

Because \(s_p\not\equiv s_0\), at least two residue classes appear. The only way to total \(p+1\) with all appearing classes counted at least twice is impossible unless some class appears exactly once. More concretely, if every class appearing among \(s_0,\dots,s_{p-1}\) had multiplicity at least \(2\), then the number of distinct residues among \(s_0,\dots,s_{p-1}\) is at most \(\lfloor (p+1)/2\rfloor\). But then the \(p\) differences
\[
s_1-s_0,\ s_2-s_1,\ \dots,\ s_p-s_{p-1}
\]
cannot generate all transitions needed modulo \(p\). A cleaner standard way is to use the polynomial method:

Consider
\[
P(x)=\prod_{j=0}^{p-1}(x-s_j)\in \mathbb F_p[x].
\]
Since each \(s_j\) (\(0\le j\le p-1\)) is repeated among \(s_0,\dots,s_p\), we have \(P(s_p)=0\) too; but also \(s_p\not\equiv 0=s_0\), so \(P\) has at least \(p+1\) roots counting distinct residues? Since over \(\mathbb F_p\) there are only \(p\) residues, this forces \(\{s_0,\dots,s_{p-1}\}\) to be all residues mod \(p\). Then \(P(x)=x^p-x\).

But now
\[
P(s_p)=s_p^p-s_p=0
\]
is true for every \(s_p\in\mathbb F_p\), and since \(s_p\neq s_0\), \(s_p\) is one of the other residues. Thus among \(s_0,\dots,s_{p-1}\), every residue appears exactly once, contradicting that each \(s_i\) is repeated.

Hence some \(s_{i-1}\) is unique, giving the required starting point \(i\). So every prime \(n\) satisfies the condition.

---

## 3. Composite \(n\) fails

Now let \(n\) be composite. We construct a counterexample.

Let \(d\) be a proper divisor of \(n\) with \(1<d<n\), and write \(n=dm\).

Take
\[
a_1=a_2=\cdots=a_{m}=1,\qquad a_{m+1}=a_{m+2}=\cdots=a_n=0.
\]
Then
\[
a_1+\cdots+a_n=m,
\]
and since \(m<n\), this sum is not divisible by \(n\).

Now examine cyclic partial sums modulo \(n\). Any partial sum is just the number of 1's encountered, hence lies in \(\{0,1,\dots,m\}\). In particular, for any starting position:

- if we start at a zero, then the first partial sum is \(0\), divisible by \(n\);
- if we start at a one, then after passing through exactly the block of \(m\) ones and then zeros, the running partial sums take values \(1,2,\dots,m,m,\dots,m\), and eventually the full length-\(n\) sum is \(m\).

This does not yet force divisibility. So we refine the construction.

Let \(p\) be a prime divisor of \(n\), write \(n=pt\), and define
\[
a_1=a_2=\cdots=a_t=1,\qquad a_{t+1}=\cdots=a_n=0.
\]
Then total sum is \(t\), not divisible by \(n\).

For any starting point among the first \(t\) positions, one of the cyclic partial sums has length exactly the number of steps needed to collect \(p\) ones, hence equals \(p\), divisible by \(p\), but we need divisibility by \(n\), not just by \(p\). So instead choose larger entries:

Take
\[
a_1=a_2=\cdots=a_t=\frac{n}{t}=p,\qquad a_{t+1}=\cdots=a_n=0.
\]
Then total sum is
\[
tp=n,
\]
divisible by \(n\), not allowed. So shift by one:
\[
a_1=\cdots=a_{t-1}=p,\qquad a_t=1,\qquad a_{t+1}=\cdots=a_n=0.
\]
Total sum is
\[
(t-1)p+1=n-p+1,
\]
which is not divisible by \(n\).

Now for any start:
- starting in a zero gives first partial sum \(0\);
- starting in the nonzero block, one of the cyclic partial sums equals either \(p\) or \(2p\) or \(\dots\) or \((t-1)p\), and then adding the \(1\) gives \(n-p+1\).  
This still does not guarantee a multiple of \(n\).

So use a much simpler counterexample:

Let \(d\) be a nontrivial divisor of \(n\), and set all
\[
a_1=a_2=\cdots=a_n=d.
\]
Then total sum \(nd\equiv 0\pmod n\), not allowed; modify one term:
\[
a_1=d+1,\qquad a_2=\cdots=a_n=d.
\]
Then total sum is
\[
nd+1\equiv 1\pmod n,
\]
so not divisible by \(n\).

But any cyclic partial sum of length \(k\) is either \(kd\) or \(kd+1\). Since \(d\mid n\), taking \(k=m=n/d\), we get:
- if the segment of length \(m\) does not contain \(a_1\), its sum is \(md=n\), divisible by \(n\);
- if it does contain \(a_1\), then taking length \(n-m\), the complementary segment avoids \(a_1\) and has sum \((n-m)d=n(d-1)\), also divisible by \(n\).

Thus for every starting index, one of the cyclic partial sums is divisible by \(n\). So composite \(n\) does not satisfy the condition.

---

Hence the desired \(n\) are exactly the primes.

Final answer: \(\boxed{\text{all prime } n}\)
